title: SEGMENT 17 — Limitations — when afucose isn't enough

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Limitations — when afucose isn't enough
Let's be honest about what afucose engineering, and Fc glycoengineering more broadly, cannot do.
Because every therapeutic modality has its limits, and a good scientist understands the boundaries of the technology as well as its capabilities.
The most fundamental limitation of afucose engineering is that it depends on the patient having functional NK cells with functional FcγRIIIa.
Afucose boosts ADCC.
ADCC requires effector cells.
If you don't have effector cells, or if your effector cells don't bear the right receptors, afucose engineering gives you nothing.
Consider the patient populations where this matters.
Heavily pre-treated cancer patients, who have received multiple lines of chemotherapy, often have depleted NK cell populations.
Patients on immunosuppressive regimens — including some used in transplantation or autoimmune disease — have suppressed NK function.
Elderly patients have reduced NK cytotoxicity.
Patients with primary immunodeficiencies affecting NK cells get no benefit from ADCC.
And patients undergoing allogeneic stem cell transplant may have donor NK cells that haven't yet reconstituted.
In these populations, an afucose drug may behave more like its conventional counterpart, with little of the expected potency advantage.
The clinical implication is that patient selection matters — a glycoengineered drug's full benefit may only be realized in immunologically intact patients.
This is one of the reasons obinutuzumab is positioned as a first-line CLL therapy, where patients are less heavily pre-treated, rather than as a salvage agent for end-stage disease.
A related limitation is tumor accessibility.
ADCC requires NK cells to physically contact the antibody-coated tumor cell.
In a circulating malignancy like CLL, this is easy: the malignant B cells are in the blood, the NK cells are in the blood, they meet.
In a solid tumor, with a dense extracellular matrix and limited immune infiltration, NK access can be the rate-limiting step.

An afucose antibody against a solid tumor target may not deliver the same dramatic benefit it shows in a hematologic malignancy, because the bottleneck is NK trafficking to the tumor, not NK potency once they arrive.
This has implications for which targets to pick for glycoengineering.
Hematologic malignancies — CLL, lymphoma, leukemia — are good candidates: the cells are accessible.
Solid tumors are more variable.
Targets on the surface of immune cells themselves (like CCR4 on T-cell lymphomas) are particularly good candidates, because the immune cells are inherently accessible to NK killing.
A third limitation is immunogenicity risk.

We have spent the lecture treating the afucosylated antibody as molecularly identical to its fucosylated counterpart except for one missing sugar, but in practice, glycoengineered preparations can have subtle differences in other glycan attributes — bisecting GlcNAc levels, high-mannose content, hybrid structures — that vary with platform.
Some of these unusual glycan structures can be more immunogenic, more rapidly cleared, or interact with mannose-binding lectin and other innate immune pathways in unexpected ways.
The risk is small but real, and it's one of the reasons regulators scrutinize glycoengineered drugs more carefully than conventional ones.
A fourth limitation is the diminishing returns problem.
Going from 5% afucose to 95% afucose gives you a roughly fifty-fold ADCC boost.
Going from 95% to 100% gives you... not much more.
The dose-response curve is sigmoidal, and once you're well past the inflection point, further engineering of the same parameter doesn't help.

This is why hybrid designs — afucose plus galactose, or other multi-attribute combinations — are increasingly important: you can't squeeze additional benefit out of the afucose lever once you've maxed it, but you can stack additional benefits from other glycan modifications.
A fifth limitation, and this is more philosophical, is that Fc glycoengineering doesn't help if the underlying biology of the disease doesn't depend on Fc effector functions.
If your antibody works by blockade — by occupying a receptor and preventing ligand binding — glycoengineering for ADCC is irrelevant.

Many therapeutic antibodies work by pure blockade: trastuzumab for HER2-positive breast cancer has a significant blockade component, the PD-1 and PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors work by pure blockade, anti-cytokine antibodies like adalimumab and infliximab work by neutralization.
For these mechanisms, glycoengineering has little or nothing to offer.
Even within the cytotoxic-antibody class, the dependence on Fc effector functions varies by target and by disease context.
The drugs where glycoengineering has paid off most clearly — rituximab biology, anti-CCR4 — are ones where Fc-dependent killing is a major mechanism of action.
The drugs where it has paid off less are ones where the killing comes through other routes or where the target biology is dominated by direct effects.
The lesson is selection.
Pick the right target.
Pick the right indication.
Pick the right patient population.
Glycoengineer for the right effector function.
When the design choices align, the drug can be transformative.
When they don't, the engineering may be wasted.
As with all of medicine, the molecular elegance is necessary but not sufficient.
